patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/hugetlb: fix swap entry corruption when clearing uffd-wp at fork()

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/hugetlb: fix swap entry corruption when clearing uffd-wp at fork()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's hugetlb memory management code that causes swap entry corruption when clearing the uffd-wp bit during fork(). The issue arises because huge_pte_clear_uffd_wp() operates on the present-PTE bit position, but swap entries keep the uffd-wp state elsewhere. As a result, when clearing the uffd-wp bit in a hugetlb page's swap entry, it advances the encoded PFN by two pages. This corruption is mostly latent and only manifests when re-encoding the corrupted offset or when an hwpoison entry misidentifies which page is poisoned. The patch replaces huge_pte_clear_uffd_wp() with pte_swp_clear_uffd_wp(), matching copy_nonpresent_pte() and move_huge_pte().

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v8 00/15] userfaultfd: working set tracking for VM guest memory

version_history.subject: [PATCH v8 00/15] userfaultfd: working set tracking for VM guest memory

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 01/15] mm: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers (pte_protnone and pmd_protnone) from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING, making them available independently of NUMA balancing. The capability to detect present-but-inaccessible page table entries is useful beyond NUMA balancing and is now enabled for six architectures that support it (x86_64, arm64, powerpc, s390, riscv, loongarch). The patch introduces a new configuration option CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE, which depends on the presence of protnone PTEs. This change does not introduce any functional changes and only reorganizes the code to make it more modular and flexible.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 02/15] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE bit macros to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the PTE bit macros related to userfaultfd (UFFD) tracking from 'uffd-wp' to just 'uffd'. This change is necessary because UFFD will use the same bit for access-tracking, distinct from mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA-hinting PTEs. The rename is mechanical and does not introduce any behavior changes. It affects various architectures including x86, arm64, and riscv.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 03/15] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE accessors to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames kernel-internal functions and macros related to the uffd-wp PTE bit, which is used for userfaultfd write protection. The rename is mechanical and does not change behavior. The bit's meaning now depends on the VMA flag (WP or RWP), rather than its name. This change is part of a larger series of patches that reuses the uffd-wp PTE bit for access-tracking PTEs alongside write-protected ones.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 04/15] userfaultfd: test uffd VMA flags through the vma_flags_t API
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the userfaultfd VMA flag helpers to use the vma_flags_t accessor function, which is a more modern and unified API for accessing VMA flags. The change affects several functions that check for specific uffd flags in a VMA's vm_flags field. The update ensures consistency with the new config-gated per-mode masks and maintains identical behavior as before. No functional changes are introduced by this patch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 05/15] mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag called VM_UFFD_RWP to provide read-write protection for userfaultfd. The flag is gated on the CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP configuration option and is currently not set or queried by any code. It extends the existing userfaultfd protection from plain write-protection (WP) to full read-write protection, trapping both reads and writes through userfaultfd. The patch also introduces new helpers for userfaultfd read-write protection and updates various files to include the new flag in their documentation and usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 06/15] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new flags to the change_protection() function in the Linux kernel: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE. These flags are used by userfaultfd RWP (Read-Write Protection) to install and restore protection on pages. The MM_CP_UFFD_RWP flag sets a page as read-only with the uffd bit set, while the MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE flag restores the original protection of a page and clears the uffd bit. The patch also updates various kernel functions to handle these new flags correctly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 07/15] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that the RWP (Read-Only Write-Protection) marker is preserved across PTE rewrites in various kernel paths, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect operations. Without this preservation, the RWP tracking could be silently dropped, leading to unexpected behavior when accessing pages with PAGE_NONE semantics. The patch updates several kernel functions to restore the RWP bit after modifying a PTE or PMD, ensuring that the marker is preserved across these operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 08/15] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends three Linux kernel paths outside the fault handler gate to treat VM_UFFD_RWP (User Faultfd Read-Write Protected) the same as VM_UFFD_WP (User Faultfd Write Protected). The affected paths are khugepaged, rmap, and GUP. In khugepaged, ranges carrying the uffd PTE bit are now protected from collapse. In rmap, unmap batching is capped at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP to ensure each PTE can be handled individually. In GUP, faults are forced on VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT, as RWP uses protnone as an access-tracking marker rather than a NUMA hinting marker.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 09/15] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT plumbing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd interface called read-write protection tracking (RWP). It introduces two new ioctls, UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT, which allow userspace to register ranges for RWP tracking and install/remove RWP on those ranges. The feature is gated by CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP, which is auto-selected on 64-bit kernels with certain architecture features. The patch also includes documentation updates and changes to the userfaultfd code to support this new feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 10/15] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds read-write protection (RWP) fault delivery to the userfaultfd interface, allowing userspace to track and manage RWP-protected pages. It introduces new functions for handling RWP faults at different memory levels and exposes a new UAPI command to register RWP mode. The patch also updates various kernel modules to support RWP and ensures that userspace can correctly handle RWP faults in sync-mode. The changes are designed to work with anonymous, shmem, and hugetlb memory types.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 11/15] mm/pagemap: add PAGE_IS_ACCESSED for RWP tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new category to the pagemap, called PAGE_IS_ACCESSED, which is used for tracking read-write protection (RWP) in userfaultfd. The existing PTE bit is reused for both write-protected and RWP-protected pages, but this patch introduces a distinction between the two by scoping each category to its respective VMA type. This change also updates the pagemap_hugetlb_category function to take the VMA into account. As a result, the UAPI no longer conflates write-protected and RWP-protected pages, making it easier to track and manage page access in userfaultfd.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 12/15] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd (UFFD) system, called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC, which allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write page faults. This is useful for working-set tracking in virtual machines where the VMM does not need immediate notification of page faults, but rather can reconstruct the access pattern later via PAGEMAP_SCAN. The patch also upgrades writable private anonymous PTEs when necessary to prevent COW-faulting on re-access. It requires UFFD_FEATURE_RWP and adds new functions and macros to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 13/15] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl, UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which allows a VMM to toggle between sync and async RWP modes at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC being toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This feature is useful for VMMs that need to switch between sync and async modes on-the-fly, such as during working set scanning or page eviction.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 14/15] selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds userfaultfd read-write-protect (RWP) tests to the Linux kernel selftests suite. The tests cover various scenarios, including async and sync modes, page mapping, mprotect, GUP walks, and fork operations. They verify that RWP permissions are correctly restored after closing the uffd or forking a new process. The patch also includes tests for invalid register mode combinations. The goal is to ensure that the userfaultfd API behaves correctly in different scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v8 15/15] Documentation/userfaultfd: document RWP working set tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature, specifically covering read-write protection (RWP) working set tracking. It explains how RWP works, including its fault models and semantics, as well as the typical workflow for a virtual machine (VMM) to track working sets. The documentation also covers the UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP ioctl command, which enables RWP on a memory range, and provides examples of how it can be used.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for tracking the working set of VM guest memory using userfaultfd. It introduces a new registration mode, UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP, which allows a VMM to identify hot pages and reclaim cold ones to tiered or remote storage. The approach uses PAGE_NONE on PTEs to make pages inaccessible while keeping them resident, and the uffd PTE bit to mark entries as 'userfaultfd-tracked'. This enables safe reclamation of cold pages and efficient fetching back on demand when accessed again.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the decoupling of protnone PTE infrastructure from NUMA balancing, explaining that introducing CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE allows six architectures to support protnone PTEs independently of NUMA balancing. The author confirmed that this change is functional and does not introduce any new functionality.
sentiment_signals: author confirmed the issue is resolved
review_comments.raw_body:
From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>

pte_protnone() and pmd_protnone() detect present-but-inaccessible page
table entries. This capability is useful beyond NUMA balancing -- for
example, userfaultfd working set tracking uses protnone PTEs to track
page access without unmapping pages.

Introduce CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE to decouple the protnone PTE
infrastructure from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING. The six architectures that
support protnone PTEs (x86_64, arm64, powerpc, s390, riscv, loongarch)
now select this option, and CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING depends on it.

No functional change -- the same set of architectures continues to have
working protnone support, but the infrastructure is now available
independently of NUMA balancing.

Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6
Acked-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Acked-by: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>
---
 arch/arm64/Kconfig                           |  1 +
 arch/arm64/include/asm/pgtable.h             |  7 ++---
 arch/loongarch/Kconfig                       |  1 +
 arch/loongarch/include/asm/pgtable.h         |  4 +--
 arch/powerpc/include/asm/book3s/64/pgtable.h |  8 ++---
 arch/powerpc/platforms/Kconfig.cputype       |  1 +
 arch/riscv/Kconfig                           |  1 +
 arch/riscv/include/asm/pgtable.h             |  7 ++---
 arch/s390/Kconfig                            |  1 +

diff --git a/include/linux/pgtable.h b/include/linux/pgtable.h
index 2981e386da7b..7d3dc8abc6c8 100644
--- a/include/linux/pgtable.h
+++ b/include/linux/pgtable.h
@@ -2129,18 +2129,26 @@ static inline int pud_trans_unstable(pud_t *pud)

 }
 
-#ifndef CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
+#ifndef CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE
 /*
- * In an inaccessible (PROT_NONE) VMA, pte_protnone() may indicate "yes". It is
- * perfectly valid to indicate "no" in that case, which is why our default
- * implementation defaults to "always no".
+ * In an inaccessible (PROT_NONE) VMA, pte_protnone() may indicate "yes". It
+ * is perfectly valid to indicate "no" in that case, which is why our
+ * default implementation defaults to "always no".
  *
- * In an accessible VMA, however, pte_protnone() reliably indicates PROT_NONE
- * page protection due to NUMA hinting. NUMA hinting faults only apply in
- * accessible VMAs.
+ * In an accessible VMA, pte_protnone() reliably indicates a present
+ * PROT_NONE page protection. Today the kernel uses such PTEs for two
+ * purposes: NUMA hinting faults, and userfaultfd RWP tracking on
+ * VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs. The two are distinguished by the uffd PTE bit and
+ * the VMA flag; see include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h.
  *
- * So, to reliably identify PROT_NONE PTEs that require a NUMA hinting fault,
- * looking at the VMA accessibility is sufficient.
+ * So, to reliably identify PROT_NONE PTEs that require kernel handling,
+ * looking at the VMA accessibility (and the uffd bit on RWP VMAs) is
+ * sufficient.
+ *
+ * Architectures without CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE get the always-zero
+ * stubs below; PAGE_NONE references that survive to runtime fire the
+ * BUILD_BUG() fallback, since callers should have folded such paths to
+ * dead code via IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE).
  */
 static inline int pte_protnone(pte_t pte)
 {
@@ -2151,7 +2159,11 @@ static inline int pmd_protnone(pmd_t pmd)
 {
 	return 0;
 }
-#endif /* CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING */
+
+#ifndef PAGE_NONE
+#define PAGE_NONE ({ BUILD_BUG(); (pgprot_t){0}; })
+#endif
+#endif /* CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE */
 
 #endif /* CONFIG_MMU */

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the naming convention of the uffd-wp PTE bit being renamed to userfaultfd tracking, distinct from mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA-hinting PTEs. The author confirms this is a mechanical rename with no behavior change.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the reuse of the uffd-wp PTE bit for access-tracking PTEs, alongside write-protected ones. They are renaming kernel-internal names to reflect this change, which is a mechanical rename with no behavior change.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the use of vma->vm_flags directly in uffd VMA-flag helpers, which will be replaced by the accessor function vma_test_any_mask() to handle config-gated per-mode masks.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the handling of VM_UFFD_RWP in the mm.h file, explaining that it is gated on CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP and currently aliases VM_NONE until the UAPI patch is introduced. The author confirmed that nothing sets or queries the flag yet.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>

Preparatory patch for userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP). RWP
extends userfaultfd protection from plain write-protection (WP) to
full read-write protection: accesses to an RWP-protected range --
reads as well as writes -- trap through userfaultfd.

Reserve VM_UFFD_RWP, add the userfaultfd_rwp() and
userfaultfd_protected() helpers, and wire up the smaps "ur" entry and
the trace-flag table the rest of the series will use. The flag is
gated on CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP, which is introduced together with the
UAPI in a later patch; until then VM_UFFD_RWP aliases VM_NONE and
every downstream check folds to dead code.

Nothing sets or queries the flag yet.

Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6
Reviewed-by: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>
Reviewed-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>
---
 Documentation/filesystems/proc.rst |  1 +
 fs/proc/task_mmu.c                 |  3 +++
 include/linux/mm.h                 | 41 ++++++++++++++++++++----------
 include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h      | 32 +++++++++++++++++++----
 include/trace/events/mmflags.h     |  7 +++++
 5 files changed, 65 insertions(+), 19 deletions(-)

diff --git a/Documentation/filesystems/proc.rst b/Documentation/filesystems/proc.rst
index 2ccd5b2dfdd3..27189f6e004e 100644
--- a/Documentation/filesystems/proc.rst
+++ b/Documentation/filesystems/proc.rst
@@ -608,6 +608,7 @@ encoded manner. The codes are the following:
     um    userfaultfd missing tracking
     uw    userfaultfd wr-protect tracking
     ui    userfaultfd minor fault
+    ur    userfaultfd read-write-protect tracking
     ss    shadow/guarded control stack page
     sl    sealed
     lf    lock on fault pages
diff --git a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
index 56deb516b193..5b55c59573a3 100644
--- a/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
+++ b/fs/proc/task_mmu.c
@@ -1237,6 +1237,9 @@ static void show_smap_vma_flags(struct seq_file *m, struct vm_area_struct *vma)
 #ifdef CONFIG_HAVE_ARCH_USERFAULTFD_MINOR
 		[ilog2(VM_UFFD_MINOR)]	= "ui",
 #endif /* CONFIG_HAVE_ARCH_USERFAULTFD_MINOR */
+#ifdef CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP
+		[ilog2(VM_UFFD_RWP)]	= "ur",
+#endif
 #ifdef CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_USER_SHADOW_STACK
 		[ilog2(VM_SHADOW_STACK)] = "ss",
 #endif
diff --git a/include/linux/mm.h b/include/linux/mm.h
index 485df9c2dbdd..5ac31fbadeef 100644
--- a/include/linux/mm.h
+++ b/include/linux/mm.h
@@ -353,6 +353,7 @@ enum {
 #endif
 	DECLARE_VMA_BIT(UFFD_MINOR, 41),
 	DECLARE_VMA_BIT(SEALED, 42),
+	DECLARE_VMA_BIT(UFFD_RWP, 43),
 	/* Flags that reuse flags above. */
 	DECLARE_VMA_BIT_ALIAS(PKEY_BIT0, HIGH_ARCH_0),
 	DECLARE_VMA_BIT_ALIAS(PKEY_BIT1, HIGH_ARCH_1),
@@ -496,12 +497,17 @@ enum {
 #else
 #define VM_UFFD_MINOR	VM_NONE
 #endif
+#ifdef CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP
+#define VM_UFFD_RWP		INIT_VM_FLAG(UFFD_RWP)
+#else
+#define VM_UFFD_RWP		VM_NONE
+#endif
 
 /*
- * vma_flags_t masks for the userfaultfd VMA flags. VMA_UFFD_MINOR is gated on
- * the same config as VM_UFFD_MINOR -- which implies 64BIT, where the bit fits
- * -- so an out-of-range bit is never fed to mk_vma_flags() on a build whose
- * bitmap cannot hold it.
+ * vma_flags_t masks for the userfaultfd VMA flags. The two high-bit modes are
+ * gated on the same configs as their VM_* flags above -- both of which imply
+ * 64BIT -- so an out-of-range bit is never fed to mk_vma_flags() on a build
+ * whose bitmap cannot hold it.
  */
 #define VMA_UFFD_MISSING	mk_vma_flags(VMA_UFFD_MISSING_BIT)
 #define VMA_UFFD_WP		mk_vma_flags(VMA_UFFD_WP_BIT)
@@ -510,6 +516,11 @@ enum {
 #else
 #define VMA_UFFD_MINOR		EMPTY_VMA_FLAGS
 #endif
+#ifdef CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP
+#define VMA_UFFD_RWP		mk_vma_flags(VMA_UFFD_RWP_BIT)
+#else
+#define VMA_UFFD_RWP		EMPTY_VMA_FLAGS
+#endif
 
 #ifdef CONFIG_64BIT
 #define VM_ALLOW_ANY_UNCACHED	INIT_VM_FLAG(ALLOW_ANY_UNCACHED)
@@ -648,22 +659,24 @@ enum {
  * reconsistuted upon page fault, so necessitate page table copying upon fork.
  *
  * Note that these flags should be compared with the DESTINATION VMA not the
- * source, as VM_UFFD_WP may not be propagated to destination, while all other
- * flags will be.
+ * source: VM_UFFD_WP and VM_UFFD_RWP may be cleared on the destination
+ * (dup_userfaultfd() -> userfaultfd_reset_ctx() when the parent context did
+ * not negotiate UFFD_FEATURE_EVENT_FORK), while all other flags propagate.
  *
  * VM_PFNMAP / VM_MIXEDMAP - These contain kernel-mapped data which cannot be
  *                           reasonably reconstructed on page fault.
  *
  *              VM_UFFD_WP - Encodes metadata about an installed uffd
- *                           write protect handler, which cannot be
- *                           reconstructed on page fault.
+ *              VM_UFFD_RWP  write- or read-write-protect handler, which
+ *                           cannot be reconstructed on page fault.
  *
- *                           We always copy pgtables when dst_vma has uffd-wp
- *                           enabled even if it's file-backed
- *                           (e.g. shmem). Because when uffd-wp is enabled,
- *                           pgtable contains uffd-wp protection information,
- *                           that's something we can't retrieve from page cache,
- *                           and skip copying will lose those info.
+ *                           We always copy pgtables when dst_vma has the
+ *                           uffd PTE bit in use even if it's file-backed
+ *                           (e.g. shmem). Because when the uffd bit is
+ *                           in use, the pgtable contains the protection
+ *                           information, that's something we can't
+ *                           retrieve from page cache, and skip copying
+ *                           will lose those info.
  *
  *          VM_MAYBE_GUARD - Could contain page guard region markers which
  *                           by design are a property of the page tables
diff --git a/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h b/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h
index c4f2cc6dfcf0..61ea83dbd026 100644
--- a/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h
+++ b/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h
@@ -21,10 +21,11 @@
 #include <linux/hugetlb_inline.h>
 
 /* The set of all possible UFFD-related VM flags. */
-#define __VM_UFFD_FLAGS (VM_UFFD_MISSING | VM_UFFD_WP | VM_UFFD_MINOR)
+#define __VM_UFFD_FLAGS (VM_UFFD_MISSING | VM_UFFD_MINOR | \
+			 VM_UFFD_WP | VM_UFFD_RWP)
 
 #define __VMA_UFFD_FLAGS mk_vma_flags_from_masks(VMA_UFFD_MISSING, VMA_UFFD_WP, \
-						 VMA_UFFD_MINOR)
+						 VMA_UFFD_MINOR, VMA_UFFD_RWP)
 
 /*
  * CAREFUL: Check include/uapi/asm-generic/fcntl.h when defining

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the change_protection() primitive used by userfaultfd RWP, specifically that it should restore vma->vm_page_prot and clear the uffd bit when resolving an RWP fault. The author agrees to restructure in v2 to address this issue.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern that the uffd PTE bit must survive any kernel path that rewrites a PTE on a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA, otherwise the marker that carries PAGE_NONE semantics would be silently dropped and the next access would leak past RWP tracking. The author has provided a detailed explanation of how they plan to wire the preservation through every relevant path, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the uffd PTE bit is silently destroyed or bypassed in three mm paths outside the fault handler gate: khugepaged, rmap, and GUP. The author extended each path to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same as VM_UFFD_WP by broadening the VMA predicate from userfaultfd_wp() to userfaultfd_protected(), forcing a fault through gup_can_follow_protnone(), or capping batching at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP. The author confirmed that per-PTE RWP state is preserved in these paths.
sentiment_signals: author confirmed the issue is resolved
review_comments.raw_body:
From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>

Three mm paths outside the fault handler gate on the uffd PTE bit
today: khugepaged (skip collapse on ranges carrying markers), rmap
(cap unmap batching), and GUP (force a fault through
gup_can_follow_protnone). Extend each to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same
as VM_UFFD_WP; otherwise per-PTE RWP state is silently destroyed or
bypassed.

khugepaged: try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp() and
file_backed_vma_is_retractable() already refuse to collapse or
retract page tables on ranges carrying the uffd PTE bit. Broaden the
VMA predicate from userfaultfd_wp() to userfaultfd_protected() so
VM_UFFD_RWP ranges get the same protection. hpage_collapse_scan_pmd()
needs no change \u2014 its existing pte_uffd() check already catches an
RWP PTE because it carries the uffd bit.

rmap: folio_unmap_pte_batch() caps batching at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP so
the restore path handles each PTE with its own marker.

GUP: gup_can_follow_protnone() forces a fault on VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs
regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT. RWP uses protnone as an
access-tracking marker, not for NUMA hinting, so any GUP \u2014 read or
write \u2014 must go through the userfaultfd fault path.

Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6
Acked-by: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>
Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>
---
 include/linux/mm.h | 16 +++++++++++++++-
 mm/khugepaged.c    | 18 +++++++++++-------
 mm/rmap.c          |  2 +-
 3 files changed, 27 insertions(+), 9 deletions(-)

diff --git a/include/linux/mm.h b/include/linux/mm.h
index 3d4d5f9a6f1b..da8a07cd49a1 100644
--- a/include/linux/mm.h
+++ b/include/linux/mm.h
@@ -4644,11 +4644,25 @@ static inline int vm_fault_to_errno(vm_fault_t vm_fault, int foll_flags)
 
 /*
  * Indicates whether GUP can follow a PROT_NONE mapped page, or whether
- * a (NUMA hinting) fault is required.
+ * a (NUMA hinting or userfaultfd RWP) fault is required.
  */
 static inline bool gup_can_follow_protnone(const struct vm_area_struct *vma,
 					   unsigned int flags)
 {
+	/*
+	 * VM_UFFD_RWP uses protnone as an access-tracking marker, not for
+	 * NUMA hinting. GUP must always take a fault so the access is
+	 * delivered to userfaultfd, regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT.
+	 *
+	 * Only do so while the VMA is accessible. If it has been made
+	 * inaccessible (e.g. mprotect(PROT_NONE)), fall through to the guard
+	 * below: forcing a fault there would loop, as handle_mm_fault() makes
+	 * no progress on protnone in an inaccessible VMA, and the access is
+	 * denied regardless of RWP anyway.
+	 */
+	if (vma_test_single_mask(vma, VMA_UFFD_RWP) && vma_is_accessible(vma))
+		return false;
+
 	/*
 	 * If callers don't want to honor NUMA hinting faults, no need to
 	 * determine if we would actually have to trigger a NUMA hinting fault.
diff --git a/mm/khugepaged.c b/mm/khugepaged.c
index 9eb03f1d1f1d..483bcebd6977 100644
--- a/mm/khugepaged.c
+++ b/mm/khugepaged.c
@@ -1892,8 +1892,11 @@ static enum scan_result try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp(struct mm_struct *mm, unsign
 	if (!thp_vma_allowable_order(vma, vma->vm_flags, TVA_FORCED_COLLAPSE, PMD_ORDER))
 		return SCAN_VMA_CHECK;
 
-	/* Keep pmd pgtable for uffd-wp; see comment in retract_page_tables() */
-	if (userfaultfd_wp(vma))
+	/*
+	 * Keep pmd pgtable while the uffd bit is in use; see comment in
+	 * retract_page_tables().
+	 */
+	if (userfaultfd_protected(vma))
 		return SCAN_PTE_UFFD;
 
 	folio = filemap_lock_folio(vma->vm_file->f_mapping,
@@ -2106,13 +2109,14 @@ static bool file_backed_vma_is_retractable(struct vm_area_struct *vma)
 		return false;
 
 	/*
-	 * When a vma is registered with uffd-wp, we cannot recycle
+	 * When a vma is registered with uffd-wp or RWP, we cannot recycle
 	 * the page table because there may be pte markers installed.
-	 * Other vmas can still have the same file mapped hugely, but
-	 * skip this one: it will always be mapped in small page size
-	 * for uffd-wp registered ranges.
+	 * VM_UFFD_RWP ranges similarly rely on per-PTE uffd state
+	 * and cannot be recycled to a shared PMD. Other vmas can still
+	 * have the same file mapped hugely, but skip this one: it will
+	 * always be mapped in small page size for these registrations.
 	 */
-	if (userfaultfd_wp(vma))
+	if (userfaultfd_protected(vma))
 		return false;
 
 	/*
diff --git a/mm/rmap.c b/mm/rmap.c
index 546bc1cf9391..9fb733489898 100644
--- a/mm/rmap.c
+++ b/mm/rmap.c
@@ -1965,7 +1965,7 @@ static inline unsigned int folio_unmap_pte_batch(struct folio *folio,
 	if (pte_unused(pte))
 		return 1;
 
-	if (userfaultfd_wp(vma))
+	if (userfaultfd_protected(vma))
 		return 1;
 
 	/*
-- 
2.54.0

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about re-registering an already-registered range not dropping per-PTE markers by explaining that the call is rejected with -EBUSY and userspace must issue UFFDIO_UNREGISTER first.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the uffd PTE bit is not exclusive to RWP mode and can be set in WP mode as well, which could lead to incorrect tracking of working-set pages. They requested adding a check to ensure the RWP flag is set before setting the uffd PTE bit.
review_comments.raw_body:
I looked through Sashiko review and queued changes for v9 (see incremental
diff below). The v9 branch is available at:

https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/kas/linux.git uffd/v9

- 05/15: uffd_disable_fault_around() did not cover RWP, so one
  missing-page fault on shmem pre-installed up to 15 cached neighbours
  as accessible ptes and PAGEMAP_SCAN reported them as
  PAGE_IS_ACCESSED. Fault-around is now disabled for RWP.

- 06/15: change_huge_pud() rejects MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_ALL next to the
  existing MM_CP_UFFD_WP_ALL check. Defensive only.

- 09/15: the PROT_NONE accessibility check lived in
  vma_can_userfault(), which unregister also calls, so RWP register ->
  mprotect(PROT_NONE) -> UFFDIO_UNREGISTER failed with EINVAL. The
  check is now register-time only.

- 13/15: userfaultfd_register() read ctx->features with a plain load,
  racing the WRITE_ONCE() in userfaultfd_set_mode(). Benign (the
  tested bit is not toggleable), now uses userfaultfd_features().

- 15/15: the documentation wrongly claimed UFFDIO_API returns the
  supported feature bitmask on EINVAL; the kernel returns the
  structure zeroed. Corrected.

The finding against 03/15 is a pre-existing bug that the rename only
made visible: copy_hugetlb_page_range() clears the uffd-wp bit of
hwpoison/migration entries with the present-pte accessor, corrupting
the swap offset. Fix posted separately:

  https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260703161833.57416-1-kirill@shutemov.name/

I believe the remaining findings are either pre-existing or false
positives. Ping me if you think something is worth discussing.

diff --git a/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/userfaultfd.rst b/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/userfaultfd.rst
index 69836e39aa0b..783d969f0e28 100644
--- a/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/userfaultfd.rst
+++ b/Documentation/admin-guide/mm/userfaultfd.rst
@@ -348,9 +348,9 @@ without ``CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP``, and architectures whose ptes cannot
 carry the uffd bit at runtime (e.g. riscv without the ``SVRSW60T59B``
 extension). Requesting an unsupported feature in
 ``uffdio_api.features`` makes ``UFFDIO_API`` fail with ``EINVAL`` and
-leaves the userfaultfd context uninitialized; the bitmask returned in
-``uffdio_api.features`` then advertises the features the kernel does
-support. The recommended probe sequence is therefore to open a
+leaves the userfaultfd context uninitialized; the structure is returned
+zeroed, so the error path cannot be used to discover what the kernel
+supports. The recommended probe sequence is therefore to open a
 throwaway userfaultfd, call ``UFFDIO_API`` once with ``features = 0``,
 inspect the returned bitmask, close that fd, then open the real one
 and call ``UFFDIO_API`` again with only the supported features set.
diff --git a/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h b/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h
index ca718e8590c5..bfbd6a59909f 100644
--- a/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h
+++ b/include/linux/userfaultfd_k.h
@@ -171,7 +171,8 @@ static inline bool is_mergeable_vm_userfaultfd_ctx(struct vm_area_struct *vma,
 /*
  * Never enable huge pmd sharing on some uffd registered vmas:
  *
- * - VM_UFFD_WP VMAs, because write protect information is per pgtable entry.
+ * - VM_UFFD_WP and VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs, because the write protect / access
+ *   tracking information is per pgtable entry.
  *
  * - VM_UFFD_MINOR VMAs, because otherwise we would never get minor faults for
  *   VMAs which share huge pmds. (If you have two mappings to the same
@@ -182,21 +183,25 @@ static inline bool is_mergeable_vm_userfaultfd_ctx(struct vm_area_struct *vma,
 static inline bool uffd_disable_huge_pmd_share(struct vm_area_struct *vma)
 {
 	return vma_test_any_mask(vma,
-		mk_vma_flags_from_masks(VMA_UFFD_WP, VMA_UFFD_MINOR,
-					VMA_UFFD_RWP));
+		mk_vma_flags_from_masks(VMA_UFFD_WP, VMA_UFFD_RWP,
+					VMA_UFFD_MINOR));
 }
 
 /*
- * Don't do fault around for either WP or MINOR registered uffd range.  For
+ * Don't do fault around for WP, RWP or MINOR registered uffd range.  For
  * MINOR registered range, fault around will be a total disaster and ptes can
  * be installed without notifications; for WP it should mostly be fine as long
  * as the fault around checks for pte_none() before the installation, however
- * to be super safe we just forbid it.
+ * to be super safe we just forbid it; for RWP, pre-faulted neighbours would
+ * be indistinguishable from accessed pages in PAGEMAP_SCAN (PAGE_IS_ACCESSED)
+ * and pollute the tracked working set, so each page must be populated by its
+ * own fault.
  */
 static inline bool uffd_disable_fault_around(struct vm_area_struct *vma)
 {
 	return vma_test_any_mask(vma,
-		mk_vma_flags_from_masks(VMA_UFFD_WP, VMA_UFFD_MINOR));
+		mk_vma_flags_from_masks(VMA_UFFD_WP, VMA_UFFD_RWP,
+					VMA_UFFD_MINOR));
 }
 
 static inline bool userfaultfd_missing(struct vm_area_struct *vma)
diff --git a/mm/huge_memory.c b/mm/huge_memory.c
index c67261f04731..4150f1bdea0c 100644
--- a/mm/huge_memory.c
+++ b/mm/huge_memory.c
@@ -2766,10 +2766,10 @@ int change_huge_pud(struct mmu_gather *tlb, struct vm_area_struct *vma,
 		return 1;
 
 	/*
-	 * Huge entries on userfault-wp only works with anonymous, while we

+	if (WARN_ON_ONCE(cp_flags & (MM_CP_UFFD_WP_ALL | MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_ALL)))
 		return 1;
 
 	ptl = __pud_trans_huge_lock(pudp, vma);
diff --git a/mm/userfaultfd.c b/mm/userfaultfd.c
index a7659b0a1147..c96cfe0871dd 100644
--- a/mm/userfaultfd.c
+++ b/mm/userfaultfd.c
@@ -2221,15 +2221,6 @@ static bool vma_can_userfault(struct vm_area_struct *vma, vm_flags_t vm_flags,
 	    !vma_is_anonymous(vma))
 		return false;
 
-	/*
-	 * RWP uses protnone as an access-tracking marker. PROT_NONE VMAs
-	 * have vm_page_prot == PAGE_NONE, so RWP resolution can't make a
-	 * page accessible -- the next access would fault again. Reject up
-	 * front instead of letting FOLL_FORCE loop on protnone+uffd PTEs.
-	 */
-	if ((vm_flags & VM_UFFD_RWP) && !vma_is_accessible(vma))
-		return false;
-
 	return ops->can_userfault(vma, vm_flags);
 }
 
@@ -3758,7 +3749,7 @@ static int userfaultfd_register(struct userfaultfd_ctx *ctx,
 	if (uffdio_register.mode & UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP) {
 		if (!pgtable_supports_uffd() || VM_UFFD_RWP == VM_NONE)
 			goto out;
-		if (!(ctx->features & UFFD_FEATURE_RWP))
+		if (!(userfaultfd_features(ctx) & UFFD_FEATURE_RWP))
 			goto out;
 		vm_flags |= VM_UFFD_RWP;
 	}
@@ -3825,6 +3816,17 @@ static int userfaultfd_register(struct userfaultfd_ctx *ctx,
 		if (!vma_can_userfault(cur, vm_flags, wp_async))
 			goto out_unlock;
 
+		/*
+		 * RWP uses protnone as an access-tracking marker. PROT_NONE
+		 * VMAs have vm_page_prot == PAGE_NONE, so RWP resolution
+		 * cannot make a page accessible again. Reject at register
+		 * time only: a VMA that later becomes inaccessible via
+		 * mprotect() must still be unregisterable, so this is not
+		 * part of vma_can_userfault().
+		 */
+		if ((vm_flags & VM_UFFD_RWP) && !vma_is_accessible(cur))
+			goto out_unlock;
+
 		/*
 		 * UFFDIO_COPY will fill file holes even without
 		 * PROT_WRITE. This check enforces that if this is a

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 7.1.y] userfaultfd: gate must_wait writability check on pte_present()

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5.10.y] userfaultfd: gate must_wait writability check on pte_present()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a double-put bug in the userfaultfd code, where the folio refcount drops to zero prematurely due to concurrent migration or swap operations. The fix involves gating the writability check on pte_present() to ensure that only present PTE bits are inspected when checking for write protection.

review_comments.author: Sasha Levin
review_comments.summary: Queued the patch for the 7.1.y series without any issues or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 16:49:08 +0100, Kiryl Shutsemau wrote:
> userfaultfd_must_wait() and userfaultfd_huge_must_wait() read the PTE
> without taking the page table lock and then apply pte_write() /
> huge_pte_write() to it.  Those accessors decode bits from the present
> encoding only; on a swap or migration entry they read the offset bits that
> happen to share the same position and return an undefined result.

Queued for 7.1.y, thanks!

-- 
Thanks,
Sasha

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to the Linux kernel on arm64 architecture to handle CPUs that are wedged and ignoring interrupts. When a CPU is wedged, it ignores stop IPIs and without pseudo-NMI, there's no NMI IPI to escalate to. The patch introduces a third rung in smp_send_stop() that signals an SDEI event on the remaining online CPUs, which firmware delivers regardless of the target's DAIF. This allows the CPU to be shut down even if it's ignoring interrupts. The patch also folds stop bookkeeping into a shared function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop(), replacing near-duplicate local functions.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 3/4] drivers/firmware: add SDEI cross-CPU NMI service for arm64

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 3/4] drivers/firmware: add SDEI cross-CPU NMI service for arm64

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the Linux kernel that allows delivering an NMI-like event to an interrupt-masked CPU on arm64 architecture using the SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) software-signalled event mechanism. The goal is to provide a more efficient way to deliver events to CPUs that are currently masked, which can help in scenarios such as system crashes or hangs. The patch introduces a new driver and modifies existing code to support this feature. It also includes documentation updates and adds a new configuration option for the SDEI NMI feature.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 2/4] firmware: arm_sdei: add SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL support

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 2/4] firmware: arm_sdei: add SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL support

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call, which signals a software-signalled event to another PE in an NMI-like manner. The patch introduces a new function sdei_event_signal() that wraps the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call and is safe to call from NMI or crash context.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Suggested merging the patch with another one in a later patch to keep functions where they are used.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 29 Jun 2026 16:07:16 +0100 Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

> From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>
> 
> Add sdei_event_signal(), a thin wrapper over the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call
> (DEN0054) that makes the software-signalled event (event 0) pending on a
> target PE -- delivered NMI-like even when that PE has interrupts masked.
> It takes no locks, so it is safe to call from NMI / crash context.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
> Reviewed-by: Douglas Anderson <dianders@chromium.org>
> ---
>  drivers/firmware/arm_sdei.c   | 12 ++++++++++++

> +++ b/drivers/firmware/arm_sdei.c
> @@ -339,6 +339,18 @@ static void _ipi_unmask_cpu(void *ignored)
>  	sdei_unmask_local_cpu();
>  }
>  
> +/*
> + * Signal the software-signalled event (event 0) to @mpidr. Does nothing
> + * but the SMC -- no locks, no event lookup -- so it is safe from NMI /
> + * crash context (e.g. the cross-CPU NMI service).
> + */
> +int sdei_event_signal(u32 event_num, u64 mpidr)
> +{
> +	return invoke_sdei_fn(SDEI_1_0_FN_SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL, event_num,
> +			      mpidr, 0, 0, 0, NULL);
> +}

> +

Same as patch 1, can this be merged in patch 3? Its good to keep functions
where they are used.

>  /*

>   * registering an event -- and the warning a failed registration emits -- on

> index b07113eeeff7..b9dc21c241be 100644
> --- a/include/linux/arm_sdei.h

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 1/4] firmware: arm_sdei: add sdei_is_present()
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new function, `sdei_is_present()`, to check if SDEI firmware is present on the system. The function is added as an optional consumer can skip registration and warning when there is no SDEI firmware.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Suggested merging the patch with another one and proposed changing `sdei_firmware_call != NULL` to a more idiomatic comparison. Also, pointed out that checkpatch doesn't like comparisons to NULL.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, 29 Jun 2026 16:07:15 +0100 Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

> From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>
> 
> invoke_sdei_fn() returns -EIO when no SDEI conduit was probed, and the
> core warns ("Failed to create event ...") on any registration that hits
> that. An optional consumer that registers an event from an unconditional
> initcall would therefore make every boot on a non-SDEI system emit that
> warning for what is simply absent firmware.
> 
> Expose whether SDEI firmware is present so such a consumer can skip
> registration -- and the warning -- when there is nothing to talk to.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
> Reviewed-by: Douglas Anderson <dianders@chromium.org>
> ---

Can this be merged in patch 3 where this function is actually used?

>  }

> + * Was SDEI firmware probed and is it usable?  Lets optional consumers skip
> + * registering an event -- and the warning a failed registration emits -- on
> + * systems with no SDEI.
> + */
> +bool sdei_is_present(void)
> +{
> +	return sdei_firmware_call;

sdei_firmware_call is a function pointer. The above is correct, but
can we make it sdei_firmware_call != NULL? I think that looks a lot better.

> +}
> +

>  {

> diff --git a/include/linux/arm_sdei.h b/include/linux/arm_sdei.h

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/4] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/4] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver interrupts across CPUs on arm64 systems, specifically targeting scenarios where the CPU has its interrupts locally masked. The current pseudo-NMI and FEAT_NMI mechanisms have limitations, such as introducing a significant performance overhead or requiring architectural support that is not yet widely deployed. This series introduces SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure) as a new delivery backend, which allows firmware to deliver an interrupt into a CPU regardless of its DAIF state, thus enabling the backtrace and crash-stop paths to reach a masked CPU without incurring significant performance costs.

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/huge_memory: set PG_has_hwpoisoned only after new folio head is established
[PATCH v2] mm/huge_memory: set PG_has_hwpoisoned only after new folio head is established

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's huge memory handling by moving the setting of PG_has_hwpoisoned to after the new folio head is established, ensuring that the flag is set on a valid folio head. This prevents a kernel BUG from occurring when trying to split a poisoned folio to order 0. The patch addresses an issue where the hwpoison state was being copied onto sub-folios before they were properly set up as heads, leading to incorrect behavior and crashes.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 00/11] mm: PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/11] mm: PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/11] mm: add PMD swap entry detection support
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for detecting PMD (Page Map Directory) swap entries in the Linux kernel. Currently, when a huge page is swapped out, it's split into 512 PTE-level swap entries. To preserve huge page information across swap cycles, this patch introduces infrastructure to detect and handle PMD-level swap entries instead. The patch adds helper functions for architectures that support PMD softleaf entries and provides no-op fallbacks for those without such support. This is the first of 11 patches in a series aimed at improving memory management and preserving huge page information during swapping.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/11] mm: add PMD swap entry splitting support
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for splitting a PMD swap entry into 512 PTE swap entries in the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) subsystem. This is done by adding a new branch to the __split_huge_pmd_locked() function that handles PMD swap entries differently than migration splits. The patch preserves flags such as soft_dirty, uffd_wp, and exclusive when splitting the PMD swap entry into PTEs. The change also includes checks to prevent potential issues with uninitialized variables in the swap branch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in fork path
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of PMD (Page Map Directory) swap entries during the fork process in Linux. It introduces a new function, `swap_dup_entries_direct`, which can duplicate a contiguous range of swap slots in one call. The patch also modifies the `copy_huge_non_present_pmd` function to 'copy' PMD swap entries instead of splitting them, preserving the Huge Page (THP). This change mirrors the behavior of `copy_nonpresent_pte` for PTEs and ensures that the PMD is stable across retries if necessary. The patch also includes modifications to handle GFP_ATOMIC table allocation failures and retries with GFP_KERNEL.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/11] mm: zswap: add range lookup for large-folio swapin
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a range lookup feature to the zswap compression algorithm, allowing it to handle large folios by scanning the covered range and checking if any slots are still in zswap. If no slot is found in zswap, the swap_read_folio() function reads from the backing device. The patch also introduces a new function, zswap_range_has_entry(), which enables PMD swap-entry consumers to make the same range decision before attempting PMD-order swapin.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/11] mm: swap in PMD swap entries as whole THPs during swapoff
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of swap entries during swapoff by introducing a new function, `swap_pmd_cache_lookup`, which classifies the swap cache behind a PMD swap entry as empty, backed by one PMD-sized folio, or requiring per-page handling. The patch also introduces a new function, `unuse_pmd`, which swaps in PMD-level swap entries as whole THPs during swapoff. If a PMD-order folio cannot be allocated, the PMD swap entry is split into PTE-level entries via `__split_huge_pmd`. This change aims to optimize swapoff performance by reducing the number of page-table lookups required.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in non-present PMD walkers
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates various Linux kernel functions to handle PMD (Page Map Directory) swap entries in non-present PMD walkers, mirroring the behavior of PTE-level equivalents. The changes include adding a new helper function for accounting swap and swap_pss, updating several functions to handle swap entries alongside migration entries, and modifying functions that handle faults, madvise operations, and memory policy enforcement. The goal is to ensure correct handling of swapped-out THPs (Transparent Huge Pages) in various kernel paths.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in MADV_WILLNEED
series_items.patch_summary: This patch enhances the handling of PMD swap entries in MADV_WILLNEED operations. It ensures that PMD-sized folios are properly prefetched and avoids unnecessary splitting of PMD swap entries. The patch introduces a new approach to handle PMD swap entries under pmd_trans_huge_lock(), which includes checking for split cache state, zswap entries, or existing PMD-sized folios. If any of these conditions are met, the patch splits the PMD swap entry and asks the walker to retry through the PTE path. Otherwise, it pins the swap device and reads the folio in at PMD order via swapin_sync(). This approach keeps the subsequent fault on the do_huge_pmd_swap_page() path and avoids order-0 readahead unnecessarily splitting the PMD swap entry.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in UFFDIO_MOVE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for moving PMD-level swap entries in the UFFDIO_MOVE operation. It introduces a new function, move_swap_pmd(), which moves the swap entry whole-PMD and re-anchors the PMD-sized swap-cache folio's anon rmap to the destination VMA. The patch also updates the dispatcher in move_pages_huge_pmd() to wait for migration on a PMD migration entry and route PMD swap entries through move_swap_pmd(). This ensures that UFFDIO_MOVE operations can handle PMD swap entries correctly, even when they are swapped out.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/11] mm: handle PMD swap entry faults on swap-in
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new handler for PMD swap entry faults on swap-in, allowing the kernel to handle large page mappings in one shot. It introduces a new function do_huge_pmd_swap_page() that resolves the entire 2 MB mapping by looking up the folio in the swap cache, allocating a PMD-order folio if necessary, and restoring soft_dirty and uffd_wp from the swap PMD. The handler also updates vmf->orig_pmd and calls wp_huge_pmd() to COW immediately when the resulting PMD is read-only but the fault was a write. If PMD-order resources are unavailable, it splits the PMD swap entry into 512 PTE swap entries via __split_huge_pmd().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/11] mm: install PMD swap entries on swap-out
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the swap-out process for huge pages by installing PMD-level swap entries instead of splitting them into individual PTEs, reducing overhead and improving performance. It mirrors the existing PTE swap-out path at PMD granularity, handling zswap and adjusting MM_ANONPAGES / MM_SWAPENTS accordingly. The patch introduces a new function set_pmd_swap_entry() to install the swap PMD, which preserves original soft-dirty, uffd-wp, and exclusive bits. This change aims to improve the efficiency of huge page swapping and reduce the load on khugepaged.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 11/11] selftests/mm: add PMD swap entry tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test suite to exercise PMD swap entry paths in the Linux kernel. The tests cover various scenarios, including swapping out and in of THPs, fork operations, write faults, munmap, mprotect, UFFDIO_MOVE, mremap, pagemap reading, mincore, MADV_FREE, MADV_WILLNEED, and swapoff. The test suite also accounts for the presence or absence of zswap, which can affect how PMD swap entries are handled. The tests verify that data correctness is maintained across these operations and that the kernel behaves as expected in different configurations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs, allowing them to survive the swap round-trip and be restored directly on swap-in without waiting for khugepaged to collapse the range later.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/6] mm: add softleaf_to_pmd() and convert existing callers
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new function, softleaf_to_pmd(), which generates a PMD entry from a leaf entry, completing the symmetry of the softleaf abstraction for page table leaf entries. The existing callers of swp_entry_to_pmd() are converted to use softleaf_to_pmd(). This is a preparatory patch to keep feature patches focused on new functionality rather than mixing refactoring with new code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/6] mm: extract mm_prepare_for_swap_entries() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extracts a helper function, mm_prepare_for_swap_entries(), to ensure the mm is added to init_mm.mmlist for swapoff to find and unuse its swap entries. The double-checked locking pattern was previously open-coded in try_to_unmap_one() and copy_nonpresent_pte().

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Commented that the function name is self-explaining and suggested it's not necessary to add a comment. Acknowledged the patch with an Acked-by.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/6] fs/proc: use softleaf_has_pfn() in pagemap PMD walker
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the pagemap PMD walker by adding a check to ensure that only non-present PMDs that encode a PFN are looked up using softleaf_to_page(). This prevents crashes when encountering non-present PMDs of other types, such as upcoming PMD-level swap entries. The fix is achieved by introducing a guard with softleaf_has_pfn(), matching the existing handling for non-present PTEs in pte_to_pagemap_entry().

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/6] mm/huge_memory: move softleaf_to_folio() inside migration branch
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the call to softleaf_to_folio() inside a migration branch in the change_non_present_huge_pmd() function, ensuring that it only converts PFN entries to folio pointers when they are actually used for migration. This prevents bogus pointer creation and potential crashes on mprotect() when swap entries are present.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/6] mm/migrate_device: move softleaf_to_folio() inside device-private branch
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the call to softleaf_to_folio() inside a device-private branch in mm/migrate_device.c, addressing an issue where the function was being called on non-present PMDs and potentially causing bogus folio pointers or pfn_to_page validation errors. The change mirrors a similar fix for change_non_present_huge_pmd().

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Confirmed the patch is correct and safe to apply, with no concerns or objections.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/6] mm: rename ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION configuration option to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF, which more accurately reflects its purpose of enabling PMD-level softleaf machinery. The rename is a pure change and does not introduce any functional changes. The affected architectures remain unchanged, with x86, arm64, s390, riscv, loongarch, and powerpc on PPC_BOOK3S_64 still selecting this option. This change aims to improve the clarity of the configuration options.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David pointed out that the description of CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION as having 'grown to gate the entire PMD-level softleaf machinery' is inaccurate, specifically mentioning device-private entries which rely on migration support and are not affected by the rename.

Reviewer David (Arm) suggested using either ARCH_HAS_PMD_SOFTLEAVES or HAVE_ARCH_PMD_SOFTLEAVES instead of ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF, and agreed that the config option rename makes sense.
review_comments.raw_body:
device-private really relies on migration support.

See do_huge_pmd_device_private() where we migrate back to CPU memory.

The whole concept is pased on migration. So that's why it currently makes sense.

So "has grown to" is not quite accurate. It's a different story with swap
entries (that can exist even without migration support).

---

Hm, that makes me wonder what it would take to make this all just be implicitly
supported by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE.

Looks like someone would have the courage to touch stuff like arc, mips and
sparc64 (+arm and x86, but that's less of a concern :) ).

So yeah, renaming the config option makes sense. The config space is the wild west.

We have

	PGTABLE_HAS_HUGE_LEAVES

and stuff like

	HAVE_ARCH_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_PUD
	HAVE_ARCH_SOFT_DIRTY
	ARCH_HAS_GIGANTIC_PAGE
	ARCH_HAS_PTE_SPECIAL

We do have a limited number of "SUPPORTS" (which is longer than HAS), but I
would either use


	ARCH_HAS_PMD_SOFTLEAVES

or

	HAVE_ARCH_PMD_SOFTLEAVES


Apart from that LGTM.

-- 
Cheers,

David

Author: Gerald Schaefer <gerald.schaefer@linux.ibm.com>
Date:   Tue Jul 29 13:16:19 2025 +0200

    s390/mm: Enable THP_SWAP and THP_MIGRATION

    After hugetlbfs PTE_MARKER support for s390 introduced region-third and
    segment table swap entries, it is now possible to also enable THP_SWAP
    and THP_MIGRATION for s390.

So, with that out of the way, could you revive your patchset?


-- 
Cheers,

David

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series is a preparatory step for introducing PMD level swap entries in the Linux kernel. It cleans up existing code by adding helper functions, refactoring open-coded logic, and defensive checks to preserve current behavior. The patches do not introduce new functionality but rather lay the groundwork for future changes. They are safe to merge independently and will be followed by a series that introduces PMD swap entries. The approach taken is to simplify and modularize existing code to make it easier to review and maintain.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Andrew Morton noted that the patch series is not yet in its final form and suggested queuing it in mm-new for early testing, as he believes it will be upstreamed in the next cycle.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 30 Jun 2026 09:34:37 -0700 Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:

> This is the preparatory part of the PMD page table swapin work. 

Thanks.

This comes reasonably reviewed, so I'll queue it in mm-new.

Why?

- Human review coverage and AI review make me believe this isn't the
  final version at all, but I do believe this is something we want in
  7.2, and getting it under test early will help to push it along.

- Getting it in there early means that others will base their work on
  material which will probably be upstreamed this cycle, so this gives
  them a more accurate base against which to work.

Sashiko did find a lot to talk about, some of it pre-existing so can
people who work on this code please take a look at

	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260630164143.1595669-1-usama.arif@linux.dev

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) noted that the comment about CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION being renamed to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF is not accurate, as device-private entries rely on migration support and the rename does not change the set of selecting architectures or gating semantics.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
> When a swap entry is installed in a page table, the mm must be added
> to init_mm.mmlist so that swapoff can find and unuse its swap entries.
> This double-checked locking pattern is currently open-coded in
> try_to_unmap_one() and copy_nonpresent_pte().
> 
> Move it into mm_prepare_for_swap_entries() in mm/internal.h and convert
> both callers so it can be reused by upcoming PMD-level swap entry code
> paths that also need to register the mm with swapoff.
> 
> copy_nonpresent_pte() previously inserted into &src_mm->mmlist rather
> than &init_mm.mmlist, but the insertion point is irrelevant, mmlist
> is a circular list and swapoff walks it entirely from init_mm.mmlist,
> so only membership matters, not position.
> 
> Reviewed-by: Dev Jain <dev.jain@arm.com>
> Reviewed-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
> Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> ---
>  mm/internal.h | 13 +++++++++++++
>  mm/memory.c   |  9 +--------
>  mm/rmap.c     |  7 +------
>  3 files changed, 15 insertions(+), 14 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/internal.h b/mm/internal.h
> index 8e0df3d1c6f9..0e3db9451e5c 100644
> --- a/mm/internal.h
> +++ b/mm/internal.h
> @@ -1956,4 +1956,17 @@ static inline int get_sysctl_max_map_count(void)
>  bool may_expand_vm(struct mm_struct *mm, const vma_flags_t *vma_flags,
>  		   unsigned long npages);
>  
> +/*
> + * Ensure @mm is on the init_mm.mmlist so swapoff can find it.
> + */

Comment fits into a single line and I don't think we need it (function name is
self-explaining enough).

> +static inline void mm_prepare_for_swap_entries(struct mm_struct *mm)
> +{
> +	if (list_empty(&mm->mmlist)) {
> +		spin_lock(&mmlist_lock);
> +		if (list_empty(&mm->mmlist))
> +			list_add(&mm->mmlist, &init_mm.mmlist);
> +		spin_unlock(&mmlist_lock);
> +	}
> +}
> +

Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

-- 
Cheers,

David

---

On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
> migrate_vma_collect_pmd() calls softleaf_to_folio() on a non-present
> PMD before checking the entry's type.  softleaf_to_folio() converts
> the entry's offset to a PFN, which is only meaningful for migration
> or device-private entries.
> 
> A PMD swap entry's offset is a swap offset, not a PFN, so the
> lookup would either return a bogus folio pointer or trip pfn_to_page
> validation on a debug kernel.  In the non-device-private path the
> returned folio is then unused (the OR short-circuits to
> migrate_vma_collect_skip()), but the lookup itself is already
> unsafe.
> 
> Move the softleaf_to_folio() call inside the device-private branch
> where the folio is actually needed, mirroring the equivalent
> change_non_present_huge_pmd() fix.
> 
> Reviewed-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
> Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> ---
>  mm/migrate_device.c | 11 +++++++----
>  1 file changed, 7 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/migrate_device.c b/mm/migrate_device.c
> index aa948db49501..36287e958b2a 100644
> --- a/mm/migrate_device.c
> +++ b/mm/migrate_device.c
> @@ -166,11 +166,14 @@ static int migrate_vma_collect_huge_pmd(pmd_t *pmdp, unsigned long start,
>  	} else if (!pmd_present(*pmdp)) {
>  		const softleaf_t entry = softleaf_from_pmd(*pmdp);
>  
> -		folio = softleaf_to_folio(entry);
> -
>  		if (!softleaf_is_device_private(entry) ||
> -			!(migrate->flags & MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_DEVICE_PRIVATE) ||
> -			(folio->pgmap->owner != migrate->pgmap_owner)) {
> +		    !(migrate->flags & MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_DEVICE_PRIVATE)) {
> +			spin_unlock(ptl);
> +			return migrate_vma_collect_skip(start, end, walk);
> +		}
> +
> +		folio = softleaf_to_folio(entry);
> +		if (folio->pgmap->owner != migrate->pgmap_owner) {
>  			spin_unlock(ptl);
>  			return migrate_vma_collect_skip(start, end, walk);
>  		}

LGTM

Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

-- 
Cheers,

David

---

On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
> CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION started life gating just PMD-level
> migration entries, but has grown to gate the entire PMD-level softleaf
> machinery: migration entries, device-private entries, and soon swap
> entries.

device-private really relies on migration support.

See do_huge_pmd_device_private() where we migrate back to CPU memory.

The whole concept is pased on migration. So that's why it currently makes sense.

So "has grown to" is not quite accurate. It's a different story with swap
entries (that can exist even without migration support).


> Rename CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD
> _SOFTLEAF to make this clear.  This is a pure rename: the set of
> selecting architectures (x86, arm64, s390, riscv, loongarch, and
> powerpc on PPC_BOOK3S_64) and the gating semantics are unchanged.

Hm, that makes me wonder what it would take to make this all just be implicitly
supported by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE.

Looks like someone would have the courage to touch stuff like arc, mips and
sparc64 (+arm and x86, but that's less of a concern :) ).

So yeah, renaming the config option makes sense. The config space is the wild west.

We have

	PGTABLE_HAS_HUGE_LEAVES

and stuff like

	HAVE_ARCH_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_PUD
	HAVE_ARCH_SOFT_DIRTY
	ARCH_HAS_GIGANTIC_PAGE
	ARCH_HAS_PTE_SPECIAL

We do have a limited number of "SUPPORTS" (which is longer than HAS), but I
would either use


	ARCH_HAS_PMD_SOFTLEAVES

or

	HAVE_ARCH_PMD_SOFTLEAVES


Apart from that LGTM.

-- 
Cheers,

David

---

On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
> This is the preparatory part of the PMD page table swapin work.  The
> full PMD swap entry series has been split into two parts:
> 
>   1. this preparatory series, which contains the first 6 patches. Zi [1]
>      and Lance [2] suggested to separate this out from the core series.
>   2. the PMD swap entry core series, which depends on this one. I will
>      send this once the preparatory series is merged in mm-new as v3
>      as the combined is currently at v2 [1].
>      I have not marked this prep series as v3, as its not really adding
>      support for PMD swap entries.
> 
> This series does not introduce PMD swap entries and does not install any
> new page-table entry type.  It only cleans up existing PMD softleaf
> helpers and call sites so the follow-up PMD swap entry series can be
> smaller and easier to review.
> 
> It should be safe to merge independently.  

Agreed, thanks for working on this and splitting this part out!

-- 
Cheers,

David

>> See do_huge_pmd_device_private() where we migrate back to CPU memory.

>> The whole concept is pased on migration. So that's why it currently makes sense.
>>
>> So "has grown to" is not quite accurate. It's a different story with swap
>> entries (that can exist even without migration support).
>>
>>
>>> Rename CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD
>>> _SOFTLEAF to make this clear.  This is a pure rename: the set of
>>> selecting architectures (x86, arm64, s390, riscv, loongarch, and
>>> powerpc on PPC_BOOK3S_64) and the gating semantics are unchanged.
>>
>> Hm, that makes me wonder what it would take to make this all just be implicitly
>> supported by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE.
>>
>> Looks like someone would have the courage to touch stuff like arc, mips and
>> sparc64 (+arm and x86, but that's less of a concern :) ).
> 
> You mean me back in 2018[1]? ;)
> 
> At least I got a no go for s390.

hah! and 8 years later it seems to exist upstream :)

commit 10dd5a0009898ba35eafeb6087e5c83b84742ff1
Author: Gerald Schaefer <gerald.schaefer@linux.ibm.com>
Date:   Tue Jul 29 13:16:19 2025 +0200

    s390/mm: Enable THP_SWAP and THP_MIGRATION

    After hugetlbfs PTE_MARKER support for s390 introduced region-third and
    segment table swap entries, it is now possible to also enable THP_SWAP
    and THP_MIGRATION for s390.

So, with that out of the way, could you revive your patchset?


-- 
Cheers,

David

---

On 7/2/26 19:08, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 05:57:34PM +0100, Mark Brown wrote:
>> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 05:53:48PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
>>
>>
>>> Well we're very grateful for what coverage it has :) and that's nice to have it
>>> work with email like that also, makes life simpler.
>>
>>> Mark - are you running tests based on mm-new? I'm not sure where things stand on
>>> that?
>>
>> I currently test -next, mainline and stable plus my own stuff.
> 
> Ack thanks!
> 
> I guess in the long run then we can figure out a way to not necessarily even
> require mm-new at all, and instead let our CI try to handle as much as it can
> before -> linux-next :)
> 
> As that'd be the nicest way and help reduce confusion about branches for
> mm-next.

If we go the for-test route, we'd still have a branch where people/bots/tools
can do more elaborate tests when throwing stuff together. I still consider that
reasonable, but we'll have to see if we really need that.

-- 
Cheers,

David

The reviewer also mentioned that someone would need to touch architectures like arc, mips, and sparc64 (+arm and x86) to enable implicit support.

>
> Looks like someone would have the courage to touch stuff like arc, mips and

You mean me back in 2018[1]? ;)

At least I got a no go for s390.

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Mike Rapoport questioned the purpose of mm-new branch and its relation to CI testing, pointing out that CI grabs patches from patchwork regardless of their presence in mm-new or not.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 11:48:24AM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> +cc Mike re: CI.
> 
> On Wed, Jul 01, 2026 at 04:46:13PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> > On Wed, 1 Jul 2026 09:04:33 +0100 Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org> wrote:
> >
> > > On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 12:50:24PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> > > > On Tue, 30 Jun 2026 09:34:37 -0700 Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:
> > > >
> > > > > This is the preparatory part of the PMD page table swapin work.
> > > >
> > > > Thanks.
> > > >
> > > > This comes reasonably reviewed, so I'll queue it in mm-new.
> > > >
> > > > Why?
> > > >
> > > > - Human review coverage and AI review make me believe this isn't the
> > > >   final version at all, but I do believe this is something we want in
> > > >   7.2, and getting it under test early will help to push it along.
> > > >
> > > > - Getting it in there early means that others will base their work on
> > > >   material which will probably be upstreamed this cycle, so this gives
> > > >   them a more accurate base against which to work.
> > > >
> > > > Sashiko did find a lot to talk about, some of it pre-existing so can
> > > > people who work on this code please take a look at
> > > >
> > > > 	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260630164143.1595669-1-usama.arif@linux.dev
> > > >
> > > >
> > >
> > > Hm, my understanding was that mm-new is where we take everything submitted for
> > > early testing?
> > >
> > > Did something change?
> >
> > mm-new has always been a few day front-end to mm-unstable.  To protect
> > linux-next from brand new material.  It serves no other purpose.
> 
> Well it only serves a purpose if it gives us better testing.
> 
> We have a CI running tests now though, Mike - is that predicated on material
> being in mm-new or does it just grab the email?

CI grabs patches from patchwork and tries to apply them to one of the three
bases: mm-unstable, mm-new or the latest Linus' commit merged into mm.git.

If patches apply, the other bases are not checked. 

This patches applied cleanly to mm-unstable and successfully passed (at
least once :) )
https://github.com/linux-mm/linux-mm/actions/runs/28544575927/
 
> If the CI is just grabbing email I wonder whether there's much purpose in the
> mm-new branch at all.

I can't say that CI I run has a great coverage. I don't know what coverage
mm-new has though.
  
> Thanks, Lorenzo

-- 
Sincerely yours,
Mike.

review_comments.author: Mark Brown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Mark Brown asked about the testing coverage of the mm-new branch, specifically whether he is running tests based on it.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 05:53:48PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 04:08:03PM +0300, Mike Rapoport wrote:

> > I can't say that CI I run has a great coverage. I don't know what coverage
> > mm-new has though

> Well we're very grateful for what coverage it has :) and that's nice to have it
> work with email like that also, makes life simpler.

> Mark - are you running tests based on mm-new? I'm not sure where things stand on
> that?

I currently test -next, mainline and stable plus my own stuff.

review_comments.author: Lance Yang
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Lance Yang approved the patch, stating that adding softleaf_to_pmd() as a PMD counterpart to softleaf_to_pte() completes the symmetry of the softleaf abstraction for page table leaf entries and keeps feature patches focused on new functionality.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 09:34:38AM -0700, Usama Arif wrote:
>Add softleaf_to_pmd() as the PMD counterpart to softleaf_to_pte(),
>completing the symmetry of the softleaf abstraction for page table
>leaf entries.
>
>The upcoming PMD swap entry support needs to construct PMD entries
>from swap entries. Converting existing swp_entry_to_pmd() callers
>to softleaf_to_pmd() in a prep patch keeps the feature patches
>focused on new functionality rather than mixing refactoring with
>new code.
>
>Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
>Reviewed-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
>Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
>---

LGTM. Feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: Jens Axboe
review_comments.summary: Expressed concern that only one of two related patches was applied to stable branches, potentially leading to problems. Suggested using a Depends-on tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 7/3/26 8:05 PM, Sasha Levin wrote:
> On Thu, Jul 03, 2026 at 05:32:35AM -0700, Usama Arif wrote:
>> It looks like this patch was backported, but the preceding patch [1]
>> in the series was not bacported to the stable branches. Both this and its
>> prerequisite have the same Fixes tag.
>> Not having the prerequisite will result in a NULL derefernce.
>> Could we please add [1] to the stable branches?
> 
> Now queued the prerequisite fd38b75c4b43 ("kernel/fork: clear PF_BLOCK_TS
> in copy_process()") for 7.1.y, 6.18.y, and 6.12.y, thanks!

This is a problem. Can some light be shed on why only 1 patch of the 2
got applied? This could lead to big problems, which seems to be the
case for this one in fact.

A Depends-on could be used here, but it's pretty hard for a submitter
to do that, as the sha isn't known before it goes into the maintainers
tree.

-- 
Jens Axboe

review_comments.author: Greg Kroah-Hartman
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged responsibility for missing the prerequisite patch, explaining that it was overlooked due to a backlog of patches and personal circumstances. Suggested reviewing patches before -rc1.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Jul 03, 2026 at 08:35:46PM -0600, Jens Axboe wrote:
> On 7/3/26 8:05 PM, Sasha Levin wrote:
> > On Thu, Jul 03, 2026 at 05:32:35AM -0700, Usama Arif wrote:
> >> It looks like this patch was backported, but the preceding patch [1]
> >> in the series was not bacported to the stable branches. Both this and its
> >> prerequisite have the same Fixes tag.
> >> Not having the prerequisite will result in a NULL derefernce.
> >> Could we please add [1] to the stable branches?
> > 
> > Now queued the prerequisite fd38b75c4b43 ("kernel/fork: clear PF_BLOCK_TS
> > in copy_process()") for 7.1.y, 6.18.y, and 6.12.y, thanks!
> 
> This is a problem. Can some light be shed on why only 1 patch of the 2
> got applied? This could lead to big problems, which seems to be the
> case for this one in fact.

This is on me, I only took a "subset" of the patches tagged for stable
for this round of releases as I was facing a huge backlog of stuff
(everyone loves to wait for -rc1 for cc: stable fixes), combined with me
having travelled for 6 weeks straight for conferences which didn't allow
me a ton of time to do stable kernel work to keep on top of the pile.

The patch wasn't lost, and is still in my queue to process (along with
748 other patches) it just wasn't obvious that there was a dependancy
and that I had to take them both in order, that's on me, sorry.  This is
also why we have review, to catch things when I do something stupid like
this :)

> A Depends-on could be used here, but it's pretty hard for a submitter
> to do that, as the sha isn't known before it goes into the maintainers
> tree.

Agreed, that wouldn't really work, and isn't normally needed.

But again, maybe trying to get patches that are cc: stable into Linus
_before_ -rc1 is better?  Hey, I can dream...

thanks,

greg k-h

review_comments.author: Sasha Levin
review_comments.summary: Queued the prerequisite patch for stable branches, resolving the issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 03, 2026 at 05:32:35AM -0700, Usama Arif wrote:

> prerequisite have the same Fixes tag.

Sasha
